bins.name: Require ATT Evaluation for Large Loads
bins.desc: The filers ask FERC to extend the Order No. 2023 ATT study mandate to large load interconnection. They cite FERC's own finding that omitting ATTs produces unjust rates, and argue that ATTs' speed and cost advantages make them essential to any large load rulemaking.
quotes: ATTs can rapidly increase the grid's capacity in 18-36 months using existing infrastructure and rights-of-way ("ROW") providing fast solutions to unlock new grid capacity while also being a bridge to bring new large loads on more quickly while new network transmission upgrades are built.

"Because the benefits of the enumerated alternative transmission technologies identified above are present across all interconnection processes, regardless of the size of the interconnection request, we find that the failure to evaluate the enumerated alternative transmission technologies results in both the pro forma LGIP and pro forma SGIP being unjust, unreasonable, and unduly discriminatory or preferential."

Order No. 2023 found that ATTs "can be deployed more quickly to be used as network upgrades in place of, and at a lower cost than, traditional network upgrades," and highlighted the numerous benefits commenters stated ATTs can provide including, "resolving thermal overloads and avoiding voltage collapse, allowing for better use of the existing transmission system, improving reliability, and reducing interconnection request withdrawals, restudies, and overall interconnection delays."

The WATT and AMP Coalitions respectfully request that the list of ATTs required to be studied in Order No. 2023 should also be required alongside the other principles proposed by the Secretary for any process for the interconnection of large loads to the interstate transmission system.

DLRs were initially included on the ATT list in the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR") for Order No. 2023. However, DLR was removed from the list of ATTs required to be evaluated in Order No. 2023, ostensibly because it was not among the technologies showing the "most potential to be useful," despite a well-developed record showing benefits in the generator interconnection process.
Additionally, the Commission could issue a separate rulemaking on the use of all ATTs to address issues such as congestion or grid efficiency and utilization in near term planning and operations.

bins.name: ATTs Cut Upgrade Costs and Time
bins.desc: The filers contend ATTs can deploy in 18 to 36 months using existing ROW at lower cost than traditional upgrades, and that DLR alone could eliminate a $400 million upgrade triggered by a 1% overload. They frame mandatory ATT evaluation as the key mechanism for keeping interconnection costs just and reasonable.
quotes: ATTs can rapidly increase the grid's capacity in 18-36 months using existing infrastructure and rights-of-way ("ROW") providing fast solutions to unlock new grid capacity while also being a bridge to bring new large loads on more quickly while new network transmission upgrades are built.

"Because the benefits of the enumerated alternative transmission technologies identified above are present across all interconnection processes, regardless of the size of the interconnection request, we find that the failure to evaluate the enumerated alternative transmission technologies results in both the pro forma LGIP and pro forma SGIP being unjust, unreasonable, and unduly discriminatory or preferential."

Order No. 2023 found that ATTs "can be deployed more quickly to be used as network upgrades in place of, and at a lower cost than, traditional network upgrades," and highlighted the numerous benefits commenters stated ATTs can provide including, "resolving thermal overloads and avoiding voltage collapse, allowing for better use of the existing transmission system, improving reliability, and reducing interconnection request withdrawals, restudies, and overall interconnection delays."

One generation developer was assessed for a $400 million upgrade based on a 1% projected line overload. Depending on the utility's rating methodology, DLR has potential to increase the capacity of that line by more than 1% in all conceivable weather scenarios.

bins.name: DLR Reduces Curtailment with Flexibility
bins.desc: The filers note that when large load interconnection agreements include load flexibility provisions, DLR becomes especially valuable by reducing the hours requiring curtailment.
quotes: If the interconnection agreement includes load flexibility, DLR will be even more valuable in reducing the number of hours requiring load curtailment.

bins.name: Restore DLR to Required ATT List
bins.desc: DLR was proposed in the Order No. 2023 NOPR but removed from the final rule. The filers argue DLR has a strong real-world record of unlocking capacity and avoiding costly upgrades, and should be required in any large load interconnection study process.
quotes: DLRs were initially included on the ATT list in the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR") for Order No. 2023. However, DLR was removed from the list of ATTs required to be evaluated in Order No. 2023, ostensibly because it was not among the technologies showing the "most potential to be useful," despite a well-developed record showing benefits in the generator interconnection process.

One generation developer was assessed for a $400 million upgrade based on a 1% projected line overload. Depending on the utility's rating methodology, DLR has potential to increase the capacity of that line by more than 1% in all conceivable weather scenarios.
If the interconnection agreement includes load flexibility, DLR will be even more valuable in reducing the number of hours requiring load curtailment.

bins.name: Separate ATT Rulemaking Suggested
bins.desc: Beyond the large load docket, the filers suggest FERC could open a separate rulemaking on ATTs to address broader congestion and grid efficiency issues in near-term planning and operations.
quotes: Additionally, the Commission could issue a separate rulemaking on the use of all ATTs to address issues such as congestion or grid efficiency and utilization in near term planning and operations.
